turns.content: I've been away from coding for five years and I'm trying to build a simple proof-of-concept graph. I thought the best way to handle this was just using a regular struct with a reference count field, but my compiler is giving me errors about borrow checking.

That's a common assumption for someone returning from a long break! The issue isn't the reference count itself; it's that you can't mutate the borrowed data while holding a reference to it. You need interior mutability to solve this, which is exactly what RefCell provides.
So I should just wrap my graph nodes in some kind of mutable container? Can't I just use a boxed struct or something similar?

Boxed structs won't help because they still require the outer reference to be dropped before you can touch the inner data. You specifically need RefCell, which is part of the std::cell module in Rust's standard library. It lets you mutate owned data even when borrowed.
Okay, so my node struct will hold a RefCell, right? But what if two different parts of my code try to borrow the same RefCell at the same time?

That's exactly the edge case you're worried about. If you try to call .borrow_mut() twice simultaneously on the same RefCell, it will panic with a double-mut borrow error. This is intentional safety at runtime.
I see. So for my graph where nodes are shared across multiple owners, I can't just use RefCell directly since they need to be shared too?
Correct! You'll need to combine interior mutability with Rc. You should wrap your RefCell inside an Rc<RefCell<T>> so multiple owners can share the same mutable cell.
Got it. So my node type looks like Node { data: Rc<RefCell<MyData> }? And when I want to modify the data, I call borrow_mut on the RefCell?

Exactly! The pattern is Node { data: Rc<RefCell<T>> }. When you need mutation, you do let mut val = node.data.borrow_mut(); and then modify val. The borrow_mut returns a RefMut which handles the interior borrowing.
What about concurrent access? If I have two threads trying to read from different nodes, does that still cause issues?

No, RefCell only protects the interior mutability within a single thread. Since Rust doesn't have true multi-threading in its standard library yet (you'd need tokio or rayon for async), this pattern assumes single-threaded execution where borrow checking prevents simultaneous borrows.
Ah, I see. So if my graph has 100 nodes each with an Rc<RefCell>, and I iterate through them to update values, there's no conflict?
That's right! Each node is independent. You can borrow_mut on any of the 100 RefCells at once since they're all different Rc<RefCell<T>> instances.
Wait, what if I try to update one node while another thread tries to read it? Or what about my async runtime?

For the async runtimes like tokio or rayon, you'd need to ensure proper synchronization boundaries. But for your proof-of-concept in single-threaded Rust, Rc<RefCell<T>> is perfect for building a shared mutable graph without runtime borrow checking panics.
Okay, so my final design will be Node with data as Rc<RefCell<MyData>>, and I'll use .borrow_mut() when needed. That should handle the edge case of trying to mutate shared state safely.
Perfect! You've got it. The panic on double-mutable-borrow is your safety net ensuring you don't accidentally create a race condition in single-threaded code.
Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to write a graph traversal in Rust but the borrow checker is screaming at me every time I try to mutate a node's properties after reading them.

The borrow checker prevents you from holding multiple immutable borrows or one mutable and one immutable simultaneously. You need interior mutability because the data itself must be mutable even when borrowed immutably by other parts of your code.
But I thought if I just use a regular struct field it should work? Why do I have to wrap everything in some special thing like RefCell?

Regular structs are immutable by default. To allow mutation while holding an immutable reference, you must store the data inside a type that implements interior mutability, specifically RefCell or Rc<RefCell>. These types track borrow counts internally.
Okay so I put my Node struct in a RefCell. But then when I try to do let ref node = &graph.nodes[0]; and mut node = node.borrow_mut(); it panics with double mutable borrow.

That panic happens because you are trying to mutate the RefCell while already holding a mutable reference. You cannot have two mutable borrows active at once. Instead, unwrap your existing immutable borrow using map_or or similar methods before calling borrow_mut.
Wait so I shouldn't just call borrow_mut directly? Is there a pattern where I can mutate without panicking?
You need to consume your existing immutable borrow first. Use unwrap_or_else or map to extract the value, then you are free to create a new mutable borrow for mutation.
Got it. So I do let ref node = &graph.nodes[0]; and then node.unwrap_or_else(|| panic!())? No wait that's wrong syntax.

Use .unwrap() to consume the borrow, or better yet use .map() if you need transformation. Once unwrapped, you can call borrow_mut on the same RefCell instance again safely because no mutable reference is held anymore.
Ah! So I do let ref node = &graph.nodes[0]; then node.unwrap(); and now mut node = graph.nodes[0].borrow_mut()? That feels safer.
Yes, that is the correct pattern. By consuming the previous immutable borrow with unwrap(), you clear the way for a fresh mutable borrow. This avoids the double-mutable-borrow panic completely.
Wait one more thing though. What if my graph has many nodes and I want to share ownership across threads? Can RefCell handle that?

RefCell handles interior mutability but not thread safety. For shared mutable graphs across threads, you must combine Rc with RefCell like Rc<RefCell<Node>>. The Rc provides the necessary atomic reference counting for safe sharing.
